# Project Info

## What This Is

An interactive, canvas-based presentation tool. Rather than traditional linear slides, content lives on a large 2D canvas that the viewer navigates through — flying, panning, and zooming between areas of interest.

## Core Concepts

- **Canvas**: A large spatial surface where all presentation elements are placed at specific positions and scales.
- **Scripted Walkthrough**: A predefined sequence of smooth camera movements (pan, zoom) that guides the viewer through the narrative.
- **Interactive Focus**: Any element can be clicked/tapped to expand full-screen with additional detail, independent of the scripted path.
- **Visual Journey**: Abstract generative shapes and patterns in the background create depth through parallax, reinforcing the sense of spatial movement.

## Current Status

Technical platform scaffolded. Working Svelte 5 + Vite application with:
- Camera system (pan/zoom via CSS transforms, animated with GSAP)
- Canvas element positioning and lazy-loaded content components
- Walkthrough engine (declarative step sequence, play/pause/next/prev)
- Focus system (click-to-expand fullscreen overlay with interactive components)
- Generative parallax background (Canvas 2D)
- Keyboard navigation (arrows, spacebar, escape)
- 3 placeholder slides (Title, Intro, Interactive Demo)

# Initial Prompt

I want to create a presentation that has a few features:
- I want to think of an overall 'canvas' onto which the different presentation elements are mapped - a visual story so to speak
- I want to be able to script a 'walk through' through this space, so, being able to smoothly fly from one area to the next - panning, zooming.
- I want to be able to focus some elements interactively (so outside of the 'scripted' walkthrough) - when they become focused, they become full-screen and expand more detailed info
- I want to use very modern fonts, and a minimalist, stylish design
- I want to use subtle abstract shapes and generative patterns in the background that have parallax effect to emphasise the visual journey

A few important notes for the CLAUDE.md:
- I want to keep a clear log book of what YOU (claude) have contributed, and what I (the human) have contributed. Everything I prompt and everything you do should go into this log in form of a summarised item. This dialog should be kept ongoing and never be 'compressed'
- Additionally, I want to leverage git to frequently commit - so when YOU make changes, commit them frequently in meaningful batches (even though I might override later) - if you see I have made changes manually to the code and if those changes weren't made by you and tag them such that we can distinguish commits between 'you' and 'me' - if you are not sure which changes are mine, ask me.

We also want to maintain the following documents:
- A high level description project_info.md of this development (no technical details, just a living summary of what this is growing into as we develop)
- A document technical_info.md with technical information (logging the tech stack, patterns, notes and guides around technical aspects)
- A document design_guide.md with any information relating to design and visual / UX aspects
- A document key_decisions.md which logs any major decisions we have made in the process of creation.
- A document key_learnings.md which logs insights we have during the process

Next steps:
- Create a document initial_prompt.md with this exact prompt.
- Set up all mentioned documents with their initial content.
- Initialise a local git repository and make an initial commit.

The creation of Interactie Media (IM) require a series of closely interlinked skills, most importantly design skills, technical skills and writing skills (e.g. for producting content, documentation and report writing). The majority of creative practitioners (including students!) are *not* equally skilled and passionate across all three categories. As humans, we tend to specialise around what we love to do and where we feel our talent sits. As a consequence, specialised roles are commonly found in many IM production teams, including dedicated UX designers, digital artists, front-end developers, content producers, backend-developers, UX engineers and project managers. However, and increasing number of creators work independently - this applies to you as students doing your individual project or course work, but often presists into freelance mode of working in professional practice.

Enter *agentic AI*. Unlike the earlier generation of AI assistants that behaved as autocompletes or chatbots, agentic systems can now plan, act, and iterate across each of these specialisms. They sketch interfaces, generate assets, write and refactor code, produce and edit copy, draft documentation, critique work, and follow multi-step tasks with minimal hand-holding. For a solo creator, this is a meaningful shift: you suddenly have access to something that increasingly behaves like a small production team on tap — a design collaborator, a code buddy, a content producer, a technical peer to think out loud with.

The implications for Interactive Media are significant, and they reach into every part of how we work:

- **How IM is produced**: the ceiling on what a single creator can realistically ship has moved. Ambitious prototypes, playable concepts, and full interactive experiences are coming within reach of individuals and very small teams.
- **How we learn new skills**: rather than learning one discipline in isolation before touching the next, you can now work end-to-end from day one, with an agent that explains, demonstrates, and fills in the gaps while you grow into them.
- **How careers develop**: the shape of the "T-shaped" creator is changing. Depth in your chosen area still matters — agentic tools extend your effective surface area across the others, and the creators who learn to direct them well will have an edge.
- **How we work in teams**: agents are becoming team members with roles, responsibilities, and review loops of their own, changing the size, composition, and rhythm of production teams.
- **How we create**: the creative process itself is shifting — from solitary execution to an ongoing dialogue with a capable, tireless collaborator that is always ready to try the next version.

This presentation is about exactly that: what agentic AI is actually doing inside Interactive Media production today, what's real and what's still hype, how to work effectively alongside these tools, and what it all means for you — as a student shaping your project work now, and as a creative practitioner heading into the industry next.

**Come along. Bring your scepticism and your curiosity.**

Append the current session to `docs/session_log.md` and commit concluded session audits.

Steps:
1. Read `docs/session_log.md` to find the last session number and determine the next one.
2. List `session_audits/` (repo root) to find the audit file for this session (match by session ID or most recent file).
3. Review the conversation so far and identify all meaningful contributions from both Human and Claude.
4. Append a new session section to `docs/session_log.md` with:
   - Session heading: `## Session N — YYYY-MM-DD`
   - Audit file link: `**Audit file**: [path](path)` — note that `docs/session_log.md` is one level below the repo root, so audit links must be `../session_audits/...`
   - Contribution table with numbered rows continuing from the last session's numbering
5. Commit the updated `docs/session_log.md`.
6. Commit concluded session audit files (see below).

## Cleaning up stale `.start` markers

Before committing, detect and clean up stale `.start` files — orphans left behind by sessions that crashed or were killed without triggering the cleanup hook.

1. Identify the current session's `.start` file (matches the current audit's 8-char ID).
2. For every OTHER `.start` file, check the modification time of its corresponding `.jsonl` (if one exists):
   - If no `.jsonl` exists at all → **stale** (session never produced output).
   - If the `.jsonl` hasn't been modified in the last 10 minutes → **stale** (session is no longer writing).
   - Otherwise → treat as active (another session may be running concurrently).
3. Delete any stale `.start` files and report which ones were cleaned up.

## Committing concluded session audits

After cleanup, automatically commit any concluded audit files:

1. List all `.jsonl` files in `session_audits/`.
2. For each `.jsonl` file, extract the 8-char session ID from the filename (pattern: `YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS_{id}.jsonl`).
3. Check if a matching `.start` file still exists (pattern: `.{uuid}.start` where the UUID starts with that 8-char ID). If a `.start` file exists, the session is still active — skip it.
4. Stage and commit all concluded (no `.start` file) `.jsonl` files that have uncommitted changes, in a single commit with message: `Log concluded session audits`.
5. Do NOT commit or stage `.start` files, `.gitkeep`, or any active session's `.jsonl`.

## Notes

- If a session audit file hasn't been generated yet (session still active), note the expected filename pattern (`YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS_{8-char-id}.jsonl`) and leave the link to be confirmed.
- For retrospective logging: if asked to log a past session, read the audit JSONL file to reconstruct contributions.

Agentic AI for Interactive Media Creation

Interactive Media (IM) production draws on a wide range of tightly interlinked skills, including design, engineering, and writing. Almost no-one is equally strong across all of them. As humans we specialise around what we love and where our talent sits, and the rest we muddle through, collaborate on, or avoid.

Enter agentic AI. Unlike earlier AI assistants that behaved as autocompletes or chatbots, agentic systems now plan, act, and iterate across every one of these specialisms - sketching interfaces, generating assets, writing and refactoring code, drafting and editing copy, critiquing work, following multi-step tasks with minimal hand-holding. A design collaborator, a code buddy, a content producer, a technical peer to think out loud with - on tap.

The implications are significant and reach into every part of how we work - how Interactive Media is produced, how we learn new skills, how careers develop, how teams are formed, and how we create.

This presentation is about exactly that: what agentic AI is really doing in IM production today, what's real and what's still hype, and how to work with these tools effectively *and responsibly* - where the ethical lines sit and what counts as right use, in your project work now and in the industry next.

Come along. Bring your scepticism and your curiosity.
